items.q: Did the New Deal end the Great Depression?
items.correct: It eased unemployment and restored confidence but did not end the Depression — WWII spending did. Roosevelt's reforms reshaped American government permanently.
items.misconception: Roosevelt's New Deal ended the Great Depression by 1936.
items.random_wrong: The New Deal was a treaty with Britain to share gold reserves.
items.why: Unemployment stayed high until war mobilisation in 1941. Students credit FDR's programmes with the full recovery; historians stress that war ended the slump.

items.q: Did the Wall Street Crash cause the Great Depression?
items.correct: It triggered it but the underlying causes were over-production, agricultural slump, weak banking regulation, and uneven wealth — the Crash exposed structural problems.
items.misconception: The Wall Street Crash on its own caused the Great Depression.
items.random_wrong: The Wall Street Crash happened because the US joined WWII.
items.why: The 1920s boom hid weaknesses (unequal wealth, fragile banks). The Crash was a symptom and accelerant, not a sole cause — important for understanding the New Deal.

items.q: Why did Lenin introduce the NEP in 1921?
items.correct: War Communism had collapsed the economy and provoked the Kronstadt revolt; the NEP allowed limited private trade to restore production and stabilise Bolshevik rule.
items.misconception: The NEP was Lenin's plan to permanently switch Russia to capitalism.
items.random_wrong: The NEP was introduced by Stalin to fund the Five-Year Plans.
items.why: Lenin called the NEP a strategic retreat, not a destination. Students miss the tension between socialist ideology and pragmatic recovery — central to understanding 1920s USSR.

items.q: In which month and year did the Wall Street Crash occur?
items.why: Correct answer: October 1929. “October 1932” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which 1935 Act established old-age pensions and unemployment insurance?
items.why: Correct answer: Social Security Act. “Wagner Act” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the Wall Street Crash occur?
items.why: Exact answer: 1929. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
